introduction.p: Kaylin is driving on the highway, and presently has 14 gal of gasoline in her tank. Her car, under ideal conditions, uses gas at a rate of 0.04 gal/mi. When the tank reaches only one gallon of gas, the low gas light will turn on and Kaylin will start looking for a gas station. How far will she drive before this happens?

statement.p: Write an inequality to represent this situation, using x to represent how many miles Kaylin might drive before the low gas light turns on.

solution.p: This is a rate exercise, and we can use

The question mark in the above is not an equal sign, because we are trying to set up an inequality. The initial value for the gas in her tank is 14\text{,} Kaylin is losing gas (so we will subtract, not add), the rate is 0.04\text{,} and the gas amount we are interested in reaching is 1\text{.} We do not know far Kaylin can drive until the gas gets that low, and we decide to use x to represent that distance. The rate of gas consumption we are using is only valid under ideal conditions. So the quantity 14 - 0.04 x will be greater than or equal to the actual amount of gas left in the tank after driving x miles. And we want this to reach 1\text{.} So the inequality we set up is:

statement.p: Solve this inequality. At most how far will Kaylin drive before the low gas light turns on?

solution.p: So Kaylin can drive at most {325} miles.

statement.p: Use interval notation to express the number of miles Kaylin might drive before the low gas light turns on.

solution.p: Kaylin can drive at most {325} miles, so the right end of the interval notation for this interval will be {325}\text{,} with a bracket. We know Kaylin has more than 1 gallon of gas when this all started, so she can drive at least some positive number of miles. So the interval notation, accounting for the context of this exercise, is {\left(0,325\right]}\text{.}
